narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to predict if someone will pass on their first try. For you, the model estimated a 54% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest reason for this prediction was your LSAT score of 18, which is well below the typical student's score of around 37 and strongly pulled the model's view toward expecting a failure. On a more positive note, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 7—higher than the average of about 6—pushed the prediction toward success, showing your early strong performance. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, though slightly under the average of 3.21, still contributed helpfully in the model's eyes compared to weaker profiles. However, your Year 3 decile of 2, much lower than the typical 5.5, worked against you by signaling a concerning drop in performance later on. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket and full-time study plan—which aligns with most students at 93%—provided smaller boosts toward passing but weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students because of that low LSAT and the dip in later grades, even with your solid start and personal strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's just how these key metrics compared. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying with updated highlights of your resilience and background. Many have turned similar predictions around— you've got this, and resources like LSAT prep programs tailored for underrepresented students can make a real difference. Keep pushing; your story deserves to be told in the courtroom.
